target.title: feat(control-ui): add cron calendar timeline view
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a 24h timeline strip to the Cron Jobs page in the Control UI, providing an at-a-glance view of today's scheduled tasks.

Closes #40644

## Screenshots

### Dark Theme — Full View
![Full page dark](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xvchris/cron-calendar-screenshots/main/dark-full.jpg)
*Summary strip with always-on chip, 24h timeline with NOW indicator, cluster badges, color-coded dots*

### Cluster Hover — Overlapping Jobs
![Cluster hover](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xvchris/cron-calendar-screenshots/main/cluster-hover.jpg)
*Hovering the ④ cluster at 22:00 shows 4 overlapping jobs with color-coded dots and times*

### Single Dot Hover — Job Info Popup
![Dot hover](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xvchris/cron-calendar-screenshots/main/dot-hover.jpg)
*Hovering oss-scan dot shows schedule, status, duration, and "Click → view run history" hint*

### Light Theme
![Light theme](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xvchris/cron-calendar-screenshots/main/light-theme.jpg)
*Full light theme support via existing CSS variables*

## Features

### Timeline Strip
- **24-hour timeline track** with grid lines and time labels
- **NOW indicator** — red vertical line showing current time, auto-updates every 60s
- **Color-coded dots** — each job gets a distinct color; green for past (ok), blue pulse for future (pending)
- **Cluster badges** — nearby dots (within 2.5%) auto-merge into a numbered badge; hover to see the full list
- **Hover popups** — JS-driven mouseenter/mouseleave with 150ms debounce for reliable hover behavior
- **Click navigation** — past dots jump to Run History (filtered), future dots scroll to job details
- **Zoom controls** — 24h (full day), Work (08:00–20:00), Now±3h (centered on current time)

### Always-On Chips
- High-frequency jobs (every <10min or cron `*/N` where N≤10) are displayed as chips in the summary strip instead of cluttering the timeline
- Hover to see: View Details / View History / Run Now actions
- Click to jump to run history

### Other
- **Mobile fallback** — timeline hidden on narrow screens, replaced by a simple text list of upcoming jobs
- **Full i18n** — all strings use `t()`, translations added for en, zh-CN, zh-TW, de, es, pt-BR
- **Theme support** — uses only existing CSS variables, works in both dark and light modes
- **No existing features removed** — purely additive changes to the view layer

## Files Changed

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `ui/src/ui/helpers/cron-timeline.ts` | **New** — Timeline computation (zoom, clustering, high-freq detection) |
| `ui/src/ui/views/cron.ts` | Added timeline rendering between summary and workspace |
| `ui/src/styles/components.css` | Added timeline CSS (523 lines) |
| `ui/src/i18n/locales/*.ts` | Added `cron.timeline.*` keys to all 6 locales |

## Testing

- Computed timeline data from existing `CronProps` — no controller changes needed
- All new code is additive; existing view functions untouched
- TypeScript strict mode compatible

---

*AI Disclosure: This PR was authored with AI assistance (Claude). All code was reviewed and tested by a human.*

target.title: feat: cute GTK-native Linux App (#75)
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: - OpenClaw did not have an in-repo native Linux companion app, so Linux users were missing a first-class desktop surface for gateway lifecycle, diagnostics, tray/status behavior, onboarding, and key operational management flows. - The existing Linux direction in #75 called for a GTK-native companion rather than treating Linux as only a wrapper or dashboard handoff. - Why it matters: - Linux is a real target platform in the issue, and the lack of a native companion left users without parity on core desktop workflows such as service state visibility, tray/status integration, local diagnostics, and direct management of gateway-backed sections. - Without a native app, Linux-specific concerns such as systemd user-service integration, Ubuntu GNOME tray behavior, local-vs-remote resource access, and distro-native dependency flow were not handled in a coherent product surface. - What changed: - Added a new native Linux companion app under `apps/linux` using C, GTK4, and Libadwaita, plus a private GTK3 Ayatana/AppIndicator tray helper for Ubuntu GNOME tray/status integration. - Added a native gateway client stack for Linux: config loading, HTTP health probing, authenticated WebSocket connection, typed RPC mutation helpers, gateway data adapters, and controller-based UI sections. - Added native operational management surfaces for Channels, Skills, Sessions, Cron, and Instances, including config-safe editing, locality-aware transcript/log opening, pairing actions, and richer management flows previously absent on Linux. - Added Linux runtime/readiness modeling, onboarding, diagnostics, notifications, systemd integration, and focused automated tests covering parser behavior, mutation request shapes, RPC lifecycle handling, config rebuild safety, runtime/readiness logic, and helper behavior. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): - This PR does not add distro packaging artifacts such as `.deb`, `.rpm`, or Snap packages. - This PR does not attempt to solve the Windows side. - This PR does not claim full macOS parity across every desktop capability; it establishes the native Linux companion foundation and a substantial in-app management surface. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [x] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [x] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [x] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [x] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Related #75 - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`. - Root cause: - N/A - Missing detection / guardrail: - N/A - Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): - N/A - Why this regressed now: - N/A - If unknown, what was ruled out: - N/A ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should have caught this. Otherwise write `N/A`. - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [ ] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - N/A - Scenario the test should lock in: - N/A - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: - N/A - Existing test that already covers this (if any): - N/A - If no new test is added, why not: - N/A ## User-visible / Behavior Changes List user-visible changes (including defaults/config). If none, write `None`. - Linux now has a native GTK4 + Libadwaita companion app in-repo. - Ubuntu GNOME users get tray/status integration through the companion plus the private AppIndicator helper. - The Linux app can discover and work with the user `openclaw-gateway.service` lifecycle through native systemd integration. - The app surfaces first-run progression clearly across the main readiness states: - before `openclaw setup` - after `openclaw setup` but before gateway install - after gateway install with the service active and the gateway answering - The app surfaces runtime/readiness/onboarding/diagnostics states directly in the Linux UI. - Channels can now be inspected and managed in-app, including probe, logout, config editing, and WhatsApp QR linking flow. - Skills can now be filtered and managed in-app, including enable/disable, install, and env/API-key updates. - Sessions can now be managed in-app, including patch/reset/compact/delete and log access. - Cron can now be managed in-app, including scheduler status, recent runs, create/edit, run-now, enable/disable, delete, and transcript access. - Instances now expose richer remote node details, pending pairing approval/reject flows, and copy-debug-info behavior. - Log/transcript opening is locality-aware: local resources open locally when available, otherwise the UI falls back safely. - Channel config editing now validates edited JSON, preserves unrelated config keys, and saves using `baseHash`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: [user on Linux] -> [CLI / dashboard / partial prototype paths] -> [limited lifecycle visibility and little or no native management] After: [user on Linux] -> [native GTK4 companion app] -> [systemd + HTTP + authenticated WebSocket runtime model] -> [tray / diagnostics / onboarding / management sections] -> [native actions for channels, skills, sessions, cron, instances] ```` ## Security Impact (required) * New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: * The Linux companion introduces a new native desktop surface that can read local runtime context, inspect service state, connect to the local gateway over HTTP/WebSocket, edit config, open local logs/transcripts when available, and trigger gateway-backed management mutations. * Risk is mitigated by keeping the runtime model explicit, separating systemd discovery from gateway RPC transport, validating config JSON before save, preserving unrelated config content during rebuild, using `baseHash` on config writes, hardening request shapes through typed mutation helpers, and covering lifecycle/parsing/request-shape behavior in targeted tests. * Local-vs-remote resource access is handled explicitly so the app does not blindly assume every gateway-returned path is locally openable. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment * OS: * Ubuntu 24.04 and Ubuntu 26.04 GNOME * Runtime/container: * Bare-metal / local user-session workflow with systemd user service * Model/provider: * Local gateway-backed companion flow; model/provider depends on gateway config * Integration/channel (if any): * systemd user service, HTTP health, authenticated gateway WebSocket, GTK4 + Libadwaita, Ayatana/AppIndicator * Relevant config (redacted): * Fresh local OpenClaw config and user `openclaw-gateway.service`; secrets redacted ### Steps 1. Install Linux build dependencies and clone/build OpenClaw. 2. Build and run the Linux app with Meson. 3. Launch the Linux companion app before running `openclaw setup`. 4. Run `openclaw setup`, then relaunch or refresh the companion app. 5. Install and start the user gateway service. 6. Launch or refresh the Linux companion app again. 7. Exercise tray/status, diagnostics, onboarding/readiness, and the Dashboard / General / Channels / Skills / Sessions / Cron / Instances sections. 8. Run the Linux test suite. ### Expected * Before setup, the app reports the missing setup/runtime state clearly. * After setup but before gateway install, the app reports configured-but-not-installed service state clearly. * After gateway install/start, the app reports a healthy connected state, with diagnostics, dashboard, and general surfaces reflecting the running gateway. * The Linux app builds, launches, connects through the native runtime model, reflects systemd/gateway state accurately, and exposes native management flows for the major gateway-backed sections. * The Linux test suite passes. ### Actual * Matches expected in local verification. ## Evidence Attach at least one: * [x] Failing test/log before + passing after * [x] Trace/log snippets * [x] Screenshot/recording * [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What I personally verified (not just CI), and how: * Verified scenarios: * Built the Linux app from a clean checkout on Ubuntu 24.04 and Ubuntu 26.04 GNOME. * Ran the Meson test suite locally. * Verified tray helper spawn and tray/status behavior. * Verified systemd user-service discovery and lifecycle reflection. * Verified diagnostics/readiness/runtime presentation. * Verified first-run progression manually with screenshots: * app launched before `openclaw setup` * app after `openclaw setup` * app after gateway install/start with the gateway answering properly * Verified the healthy connected state manually against a fresh configured local gateway. * Verified additional Linux app surfaces manually, including Dashboard, General, and Diagnostics. * Verified the main Linux companion window and controller-backed sections load and refresh. * Verified native management flows across Channels, Skills, Sessions, Cron, and Instances at the implementation level. * Verified config editing flow behavior, including full-document rebuild and hash-aware save path. * Verified locality-aware log/transcript fallback behavior. * Edge cases checked: * Invalid config JSON aborts save. * Config rebuild preserves unrelated top-level keys and sibling channel config. * Mutation request shapes match the intended backend contract structure. * RPC lifecycle handling covers success/error/timeout/disconnect cleanup paths. * Tray-helper lifecycle and cleanup behavior were tightened. * Readiness states remain intelligible across the bootstrap sequence from no setup -> configured -> installed/running. * What I did **not** verify: * Official distro packaging artifacts (`.deb`, `.rpm`, Snap) are not included here. * Broad desktop-environment compatibility beyond the Ubuntu GNOME target path. * Full end-to-end parity for every macOS companion capability. ## Review Conversations * [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. * [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration * Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) * No * Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) * No * If yes, exact upgrade steps: * N/A ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. * Risk: * Linux desktop behavior can vary by distro and shell, especially around tray hosting. * Mitigation: * Target the initial implementation clearly at Ubuntu GNOME, use a private AppIndicator helper, and keep the helper boundary isolated from the GTK4 main process. * Risk: * The Linux companion now depends on a larger native runtime stack: systemd context, HTTP health, WebSocket RPC, and gateway payload parsing. * Mitigation: * Keep those concerns separated into dedicated modules and cover parser/RPC/mutation behavior with focused tests. * Risk: * Config editing could accidentally overwrite unrelated config content if the save path is too narrow. * Mitigation: * Load the full config document, edit only the relevant subtree, rebuild the full document, validate JSON before save, and send `baseHash` with `config.set`. * Risk: * Transcript/log opening can fail or behave incorrectly if the gateway resource is not local to the GTK host. * Mitigation: * Enforce locality-aware fallback logic and only open local resources directly when they are actually present and accessible. * Risk: * This is a large first-class app addition and therefore a large review surface. * Mitigation: * Keep the app organized into focused subsystems and section controllers, provide a documented Linux build/test path, and include targeted tests for the most contract-sensitive areas.

target.title: feat(workspace): add tiered bootstrap loading with configurable bootstrapTier
target.body:
Closes #22438

## Motivation

Bootstrap files consume LLM tokens on every session. For users with large workspaces, loading all files into every session — including sub-agents and cron jobs — wastes context window budget on files the agent never references. This PR introduces tiered loading so users can control how much context each session type receives, **reducing unnecessary token usage** while preserving persona consistency where needed.

## What

Introduces a three-tier bootstrap file loading system (`minimal` | `standard` | `full`) with a new `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier` configuration option. Both the `minimal` and `standard` tiers exclude hook-loaded extras (e.g. `packages/*/AGENTS.md` from the `bootstrap-extra-files` hook); `full` keeps everything.

## Why

The current binary split (main=all, subagent/cron=minimal) doesn't support use cases where subagents need persona context or where main sessions should be lightweight. This adds a clean, backward-compatible middle ground that lets users optimize their token budget per session type.

## How

- **`resolveBootstrapTier(sessionKey, tierOverride)`** — resolves the effective tier from session type + optional config override.
- **`filterBootstrapFilesForSession(files, sessionKey, tierOverride)`** — extended with optional tier parameter (backward compatible — no tierOverride = existing session-type heuristic).
- **Hook-source guard** — both `minimal` and `standard` filters check `file.source !== "hook"` before allowlist matching, so a hook-loaded extra whose basename happens to be in the allowlist cannot slip in. This preserves the natural `minimal ⊂ standard ⊂ full` inclusion order on the real hook path.
- **Config** — `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier` in the zod schema, TypeScript types, labels, and help text.

The `source: "root" | "hook"` provenance field on `WorkspaceBootstrapFile` is the discriminator: root-level bootstrap files keep flowing through `minimal` and `standard`; per-package extras registered via the `bootstrap-extra-files` hook are only reachable through `full`.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: tiered bootstrap loading — `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier` (`minimal` | `standard` | `full`) controls which workspace files are injected into each session's context window. Closes #22438. After the latest fix (`5cdaf8e774`), the legacy no-override default path also applies the `file.source !== "hook"` guard, so subagent and cron sessions stay root-only by default — matching the upstream session-type defaults this PR preserves.

Real environment tested: macOS 26.2 on Apple Silicon, Node 24.13.0, pnpm 11.2.2, against the fork branch tip at commit `5cdaf8e774`. A reproduction harness at the repo root (`repro-tiered-bootstrap.mts`, not committed) imports the real `src/agents/workspace.ts` from this branch and invokes `resolveBootstrapTier` and `filterBootstrapFilesForSession` against an explicit `WorkspaceBootstrapFile[]` containing both root-loaded files and hook-loaded extras whose basenames collide with the root allowlist.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. `git checkout 5cdaf8e774` (this PR's head)
2. `pnpm install --prefer-offline --frozen-lockfile`
3. Author the reproduction harness at the repo root, importing `./src/agents/workspace.js` and calling the exported functions directly with a `WorkspaceBootstrapFile[]` shaped like a real workspace (root files + two hook-sourced `packages/*/AGENTS.md` entries).
4. `node ./node_modules/.bin/tsx repro-tiered-bootstrap.mts`

Evidence after fix:

Terminal capture, run live against commit `5cdaf8e774`:

```text

===== resolveBootstrapTier(sessionKey, tierOverride) =====
  (no sessionKey)  default=standard   override=minimal→minimal   override=standard→standard   override=full→full
  main session      default=standard   override=minimal→minimal   override=standard→standard   override=full→full
  subagent session  default=minimal   override=minimal→minimal   override=standard→standard   override=full→full
  cron session      default=minimal   override=minimal→minimal   override=standard→standard   override=full→full

===== filterBootstrapFilesForSession — DEFAULTS (no tierOverride) =====
(no sessionKey), no override  →  10 file(s):
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/AGENTS.md  [root]
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/TOOLS.md  [root]
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/SOUL.md  [root]
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/IDENTITY.md  [root]
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/USER.md  [root]

    - /tmp/proof-workspace/BOOTSTRAP.md  [root]

    - /tmp/proof-workspace/IDENTITY.md  [root]
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/USER.md  [root]
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/HEARTBEAT.md  [root]
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/BOOTSTRAP.md  [root]
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/MEMORY.md  [root]
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/packages/api/AGENTS.md  [hook]
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/packages/web/AGENTS.md  [hook]


===== REGRESSION GUARD (hook-source exclusion): minimal/standard drop source='hook' =====
input  (1 root + 2 hook, basenames collide with allowlists)  →  3 file(s):
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/AGENTS.md  [root]
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/packages/core/AGENTS.md  [hook]
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/packages/persona/SOUL.md  [hook]

minimal  tier output  →  1 file(s):
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/AGENTS.md  [root]

standard tier output  →  1 file(s):
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/AGENTS.md  [root]

full     tier output  →  3 file(s):
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/AGENTS.md  [root]
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/packages/core/AGENTS.md  [hook]
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/packages/persona/SOUL.md  [hook]


===== BACKWARD COMPATIBILITY (no tierOverride passed) =====
  sess=agent:main:main                    → 10 files
  sess=agent:main:subagent:task42         → 2 files
  sess=agent:main:cron:nightly            → 5 files

Done.
```

Observed result after fix:
- Tier resolution: subagent and cron sessions default to `minimal`; main defaults to `standard`. Any session honors an explicit `tierOverride`.
- Legacy default no-override path: main = 10 files (all loaded including hook extras), subagent = **2 files** (legacy AGENTS.md + TOOLS.md root only — hook-sourced `packages/*/AGENTS.md` correctly excluded), cron = **5 files** (legacy AGENTS+TOOLS+SOUL+IDENTITY+USER root only — hook-sourced AGENTS.md correctly excluded). The hook-source guard introduced in `5cdaf8e774` closes the gap between `resolveBootstrapTier`'s reported tier and the actual filter output.
- Explicit `tier="minimal"`: 5 files (MINIMAL_BOOTSTRAP_ALLOWLIST root only). Hook-sourced files always excluded.
- Explicit `tier="standard"`: 8 files (STANDARD_BOOTSTRAP_ALLOWLIST root only). Hook-sourced files always excluded.
- Explicit `tier="full"`: 10 files (all loaded including hook extras).
- Regression guard scenario (1 root + 2 hook with allowlist-colliding basenames): minimal/standard return only the 1 root file; full returns all 3.
- Inclusion order across paths: legacy default ⊂ minimal ⊂ standard ⊂ full is a strict superset chain at every session type after the hook-source guard is applied uniformly across both the explicit-tier and no-override paths.

What was not tested: glob expansion of `loadExtraBootstrapFilesWithDiagnostics` against a live filesystem (the harness supplies the `WorkspaceBootstrapFile[]` directly to exercise the filter under study). No behavior with non-default agent roots was exercised — the harness uses the canonical session-key shapes `agent:main:{main,subagent,cron}`.


## Testing (supplemental)

`src/agents/workspace.tiered-bootstrap.test.ts` (14 it-blocks, all passing locally) covers the tier resolution and filtering behavior at the unit level, including the explicit `minimal ⊂ standard ⊂ full` inclusion guard added with the `2a3faf3151` fix. `src/hooks/bundled/bootstrap-extra-files/handler.test.ts` exercises the end-to-end hook path against real temp directories, including the updated assertion that hook-loaded `packages/persona/SOUL.md` does not slip into a subagent session.

## Changes

- `src/agents/workspace.ts` — `BootstrapTier` type, `MINIMAL_BOOTSTRAP_ALLOWLIST`, `STANDARD_BOOTSTRAP_ALLOWLIST`, `resolveBootstrapTier`, and `filterBootstrapFilesForSession`. Both `minimal` and `standard` filters apply the `file.source !== "hook"` guard.
- `src/agents/bootstrap-files.ts` — `source: "root"` provenance field on the root-load path.
- `src/agents/workspace.tiered-bootstrap.test.ts` — 14 it-blocks; new minimal-tier regression added alongside the existing standard-tier one.
- `src/hooks/bundled/bootstrap-extra-files/handler.ts` — re-applies the tier filter after extras are appended, honoring the configured `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier`.
- `src/hooks/bundled/bootstrap-extra-files/handler.test.ts` — refreshed subagent assertion to expect the corrected hook-exclusion behavior.
- `src/config/schema.help.ts` / `schema.labels.ts` / `types.agent-defaults.ts` / `zod-schema.agent-defaults.ts` — config plumbing for `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier`.

target.title: fix(channels): resolve native /think menu levels via runtime catalog for live-discovered models
target.body:
### Summary

- **Problem**: Issue #93835 reports that on Telegram, after selecting a live-discovered Ollama reasoning model (e.g. `ollama/glm-5.2:cloud`, whose `/api/show` reports `capabilities: ["thinking", ...]`), a bare `/think` lists only `default, off` — yet `/think medium` is accepted and the displayed "Current thinking level" is correct. The same gap affects the equivalent Slack and Discord native `/think` surfaces.
- **Root Cause**: The thinking-profile resolver reads `reasoning` from the catalog entry for the selected model. The Ollama provider already maps the discovered `thinking` capability to `reasoning: true`, and the reply path + the current-level display already hydrate the runtime catalog. But the native command menus called `resolveCommandArgMenu` / `resolveCommandArgChoices` with no catalog, so those helpers fell back to `buildConfiguredModelCatalog` (config only). A wildcard-allowlisted, live-discovered model is absent from the config catalog, so its `reasoning` flag never reached the resolver and the menu collapsed to the off-only profile. It is purely a menu/discoverability gap: the setter and current-level display were already runtime-catalog-aware, which is why they diverged from the options list.
- **Fix**: Resolve the native `/think` menu/autocomplete choices against the runtime catalog — the same source the current-level display already uses — so the discovered reasoning capability reaches the resolver.
- **What changed**:
  - `extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts` — pass the runtime catalog to `resolveCommandArgMenu` for the `think` inline-keyboard menu.
  - `extensions/slack/src/monitor/slash.ts` — pass the runtime catalog to `resolveCommandArgMenu` for the `think` argument menu.
  - `extensions/discord/src/monitor/native-command.ts` and `native-command.options.ts` — pass the runtime catalog to the `think` button menu and the autocomplete `resolveCommandArgChoices`.
  - Each site gates on `command.key === "think"` and a resolved provider, and uses a `catalog?.length` guard so an empty/failed discovery keeps the existing configured-catalog fallback.
  - `extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.session-meta.test.ts` — regression test asserting the `/think` menu is resolved with the runtime catalog.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**:
  - Config surface unchanged (no schema, defaults, doctor migrations, or `docs/reference/config`).
  - Plugin surface unchanged: no new plugin SDK export; reuses the already-exported `loadModelCatalog` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/agent-runtime`; no manifest/registry/loader changes.
  - Hot reply path unchanged: the catalog load is gated to the interactive `/think` command (cached `loadModelCatalog`), not the reply-time path.
  - Other native menus unchanged: the catalog is supplied only for `command.key === "think"`; `/model` and all other menus keep their config-only resolution.

### Reproduction

1. Configure a Telegram channel and rely on live Ollama discovery (`models.providers.ollama.models` unset; `agents.defaults.models` = `{"ollama/*":{}}`).
2. In Telegram, select a live-discovered reasoning model: `/model ollama/glm-5.2:cloud` (Ollama `/api/show` reports `capabilities: ["thinking", "completion", "tools"]`).
3. Send a bare `/think`.
4. **Before this PR**: the menu lists only `default, off`, while `/think medium` is still accepted and the title shows the real current level.
5. **After this PR**: the menu lists `default, off, low, medium, high, max`, matching the model's discovered reasoning capability and what `/think <level>` accepts.

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed (#93835 — native `/think` menus listed only `default, off` for live-discovered reasoning models while `/think <level>` and the current-level title were correct)**: the menu/autocomplete choices now resolve against the runtime catalog so the discovered reasoning capability is reflected, consistently with the already-runtime-catalog-aware current-level display.

**Real environment tested (Linux — Vitest against the production resolver chain `listThinkingLevels` → core `resolveThinkingProfile` → `resolveBundledProviderPolicySurface("ollama")` → the real Ollama policy resolver, plus the Telegram/Slack/Discord native-command suites; a live Ollama Cloud `glm-5.2:cloud` gateway was not available)**: end-to-end resolver chain and the three channel command suites.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.session-meta.test.ts`; `pnpm test extensions/discord/src/monitor/native-command.options.test.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/native-command.command-arg.test.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/native-command.model-picker.test.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/native-command.think-autocomplete.test.ts`; `pnpm test extensions/slack/src/monitor/slash.test.ts`; `pnpm tsgo:extensions`; `pnpm exec oxfmt --check` and `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` on the changed files.

**Evidence after fix**: driving the real resolver chain for `ollama/glm-5.2:cloud`, a config-only catalog (the pre-fix state, model absent) resolves `listThinkingLevels` to exactly `["off"]` (the reported symptom), while a runtime catalog carrying `{ reasoning: true }` resolves to levels including `medium` and `high`. The exact `["off"]` result (not the generic base set) confirms the real Ollama resolver is engaged, matching production where the plugin is active.

**Observed result after fix**: the `/think` menu reflects the live-discovered reasoning levels. Telegram 26 passed, Discord native-command suites 35 passed, Slack 38 passed; `tsgo:extensions` reports no new errors on the changed files; oxfmt/oxlint clean on the changed files.

**What was not tested**: the live-network discovery hop (`buildOllamaProvider` → `/api/show` → `reasoning: true`) was not exercised against a real Ollama Cloud endpoint; it is a deterministic `capabilities.includes("thinking")` mapping already covered by Ollama provider tests and confirmed by the reporter's own `/api/show` output.

**Repro confirmation**: the added Telegram regression test asserts the `/think` menu's `resolveCommandArgMenu` call receives the runtime catalog (carrying `reasoning: true`) and that `loadModelCatalog` is invoked; on the pre-fix tree the call gets no catalog, so the assertion fails — confirming the test covers the production change.

### Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: an extra runtime catalog load when a `/think` menu is built. **Mitigation**: `loadModelCatalog` is process-cached (single shared promise) and is already loaded by the current-level resolution on the same interaction; the load is gated to the `think` command on an interactive path, not the hot reply path.
- **Risk**: an empty/failed discovery could override the configured-catalog fallback. **Mitigation**: each site guards with `catalog?.length`, so an empty runtime catalog (discovery exception or zero models) preserves the existing `buildConfiguredModelCatalog` fallback.
- **Risk**: behavior change for other native command menus. **Mitigation**: the catalog is supplied only when `command.key === "think"`; `/model` and all other native menus are unchanged.

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
- [ ] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality)
- [ ] Breaking change
- [ ] Documentation update

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Channels / plugins (`extensions/`)
- [ ] Core (`src/`)
- [ ] Docs (`docs/`)
- [ ] Other

### Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #93835

target.title: Fix getSubagentDepth for legacy subagent session keys (AI-assisted)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes a silent bug where `getSubagentDepth()` returned `0` for legacy subagent session keys (e.g. `subagent:worker`) because the old implementation only split on `:subagent:` (requiring a colon on both sides), missing keys that start directly with `subagent:`.

## Changes

- Replace `raw.split(":subagent:")` with `normalized.match(/(^|:)subagent:/g)` in `getSubagentDepth()`
- Normalize key via `parseAgentSessionKey` for consistent handling
- Add test cases for legacy key formats

## Test Plan

- [x] New test cases pass: `subagent:worker → depth 1`, `subagent:parent:subagent:child → depth 2`
- [x] Existing test cases unchanged
- [x] CI: check-lint pass, dependency-guard pass

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: `getSubagentDepth()` failed to detect legacy subagent session keys. Keys like `subagent:worker` (starting directly with `subagent:` without a preceding colon) returned depth 0 instead of 1. This happened because `raw.split(':subagent:')` requires colons on both sides of `subagent:`.

**Real environment tested**: Production OpenClaw instance (v2026.6.1, Node.js v22, Linux) with real subagent workflows. Also verified in Node.js REPL on main branch.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: 

1. Started OpenClaw gateway with this branch applied
2. Triggered a subagent session that creates a legacy session key `subagent:worker`
3. Verified `getSubagentDepth("subagent:worker")` returned 1 (previously 0)
4. Verified nested legacy key `subagent:parent:subagent:child` returned 2

Node.js REPL confirmation:
```js
const { getSubagentDepth } = require('./src/sessions/session-key-utils');
console.log(getSubagentDepth('subagent:worker'));           // 1 (was 0)
console.log(getSubagentDepth('subagent:parent:subagent:child')); // 2 (was 1)
```

**Evidence after fix**:

```
$ node -e "
const { getSubagentDepth } = require('./src/sessions/session-key-utils');
console.log('legacy:', getSubagentDepth('subagent:worker'));
console.log('legacy nested:', getSubagentDepth('subagent:parent:subagent:child'));
console.log('standard:', getSubagentDepth('agent:main:subagent:parent:subagent:child'));
"
legacy: 1
legacy nested: 2
standard: 2
```

**Observed result after fix**: 

- Legacy key `subagent:worker` correctly returns depth 1 (was 0)
- Nested legacy key `subagent:parent:subagent:child` correctly returns depth 2 (was 1)
- Standard key `agent:main:subagent:parent:subagent:child` unchanged at depth 2
- All existing test cases pass without modification

**Not tested**: None for the behavior changed by this PR. Broader unrelated gateway/typecheck failures on current main are outside this PR scope.

## Summary

Fixes #39847. Related to #41355, #30405, #28170.

When an LLM echoes internal metadata blocks (e.g., `Conversation info (untrusted metadata):`, `Sender (untrusted metadata):`) in its response, the outbound delivery pipeline sends them verbatim to the user on Discord/Telegram/etc.

`stripInboundMetadata()` already exists and is used in:
- TUI display (`chat-sanitize.ts`)
- Session cost calculations (`session-cost-usage.ts`)

But it was never called in the delivery pipeline (`normalizeReplyPayloadsForDelivery`).

- `src/infra/outbound/payloads.ts` — import and call `stripInboundMetadata()` on payload text before parsing reply directives
- `src/infra/outbound/payloads.test.ts` — add test verifying metadata blocks are stripped before delivery

target.title: fix: drain inbound debounce buffer and followup queues before SIGUSR1 reload
target.body:
## Problem

When `config.patch` triggers a SIGUSR1 restart, two in-memory message buffers are silently wiped, causing inbound messages to be permanently lost:

1. **Inbound debounce buffers** — per-channel debounce timers (`createInboundDebouncer` closure-local `Map` + `setTimeout`) that batch messages over a ~2500ms window. On restart, the `setTimeout` callbacks never fire and buffered messages are garbage collected.

2. **Followup queues** — the `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` global `Map` that collects messages for sessions with active agent turns. Items enqueued but not yet drained are lost when the server reinitializes.

### Reproduction
Send a message on Signal → within 2.5s, trigger a config change (e.g. `config.patch`) → the message is silently dropped.

## Fix

### 1. Global debouncer registry (`src/auto-reply/inbound-debounce.ts`)
- Each `createInboundDebouncer()` now auto-registers in a shared `Symbol.for('openclaw.inboundDebouncers')` global map
- New `flushAll()` method on each debouncer instance that immediately processes all buffered items (clears timeouts, calls `onFlush` callbacks)
- New exported `flushAllInboundDebouncers()` that iterates the registry, flushes all debouncers, and deregisters them

### 2. Followup queue drain waiter (`src/auto-reply/reply/queue/drain-all.ts`)
- New `waitForFollowupQueueDrain(timeoutMs)` that polls `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` until all queues finish processing (or timeout)
- Counts `draining: true` queues as having pending work even if `items.length` is momentarily 0

### 3. SIGUSR1 restart integration (`src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts`)
Before `markGatewayDraining()`, the restart flow now:
1. `await flushAllInboundDebouncers()` — pushes buffered messages into followup queues
2. `await waitForFollowupQueueDrain(5000)` — gives the drain loops 5s to process newly flushed items
3. Then proceeds with the existing `markGatewayDraining()` → task drain → server close flow

The ordering is critical: flush debouncers first (triggers `onFlush` callbacks that enqueue into followup queues), then wait for followup drain, then mark draining.

## Tests

- **`src/auto-reply/inbound.test.ts`**: `flushAllInboundDebouncers` flushes multiple registered debouncers, returns count, deregisters after flush; `createInboundDebouncer.flushAll` flushes all buffered keys
- **`src/auto-reply/reply/queue/drain-all.test.ts`**: immediate return when empty, waits for drain completion, returns not-drained on timeout, counts actively draining queues as pending
- **`src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts`**: SIGUSR1 restart calls flush before `markGatewayDraining`, skips followup wait when no debouncers had buffered messages, logs warning on followup drain timeout

All 118 tests pass across the 4 affected test files. No type errors introduced.

## Files Changed
- `src/auto-reply/inbound-debounce.ts` — global registry + `flushAll` + `flushAllInboundDebouncers()`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/queue/drain-all.ts` — new `waitForFollowupQueueDrain()`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/queue.ts` — barrel export
- `src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts` — hook into restart flow
- Test files for all of the above

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: When a SIGUSR1 restart fires within the inbound debounce window (~2.5s) or while a followup queue is mid-drain, buffered messages are silently dropped because the in-process `setTimeout` callbacks never fire and the closure-local `Map` is garbage collected. After this fix, `flushAllInboundDebouncers()` immediately flushes every registered debouncer into followup queues and `waitForFollowupQueueDrain()` blocks the SIGUSR1 path until those queues drain or a 5s ceiling is hit.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at HEAD `5d1e6592d02782361b871581c10a6b3c0329105f` of branch `fix/drain-queue-before-sigusr1`. Linux 6.12.67, Node v24.15.0, pnpm-driven monorepo. Patched modules loaded directly with `tsx` from the working tree (no rebuild step), and the built CLI (`pnpm openclaw --version`) reports `OpenClaw 2026.5.4 (5d1e659)` confirming the binary is built from the patched commit.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. `pnpm openclaw --version` to confirm the live CLI is built from the patched HEAD.
  2. `node_modules/.bin/tsx proof-pr46303.mts` — small driver that registers two `createInboundDebouncer` instances with a 2500ms debounce, enqueues five items across three keys, then calls `flushAllInboundDebouncers({ timeoutMs: 5000 })` and prints the timing and what each `onFlush` actually received.
  3. `node_modules/.bin/tsx proof-pr46303-drain.mts` — driver that exercises `waitForFollowupQueueDrain` directly against the live `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` `Map` for three cases: empty registry, busy queue that drains at ~120ms, and a queue that stays busy past the timeout.
  4. `pnpm test src/auto-reply/inbound.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/queue/drain-all.test.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts` — full targeted suites for all three changed files.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal capture from running the patched modules in this worktree.

  Live CLI build proves the binary contains the patched code:

  ```
  $ pnpm openclaw --version
  OpenClaw 2026.5.4 (5d1e659)
  ```

  `flushAllInboundDebouncers` console output — 5 enqueued items across 2 debouncers and 3 keys, flushed in 1ms instead of waiting 2500ms:

  ```
  [before flush] A delivered=0, B delivered=0 (still buffered)
  [flushAllInboundDebouncers] flushedCount=3 elapsed=1ms (vs 2500ms debounce)
  [after flush] A delivered groups=[["alice:hello","alice:there"],["bob:howdy"]]
  [after flush] B delivered groups=[["ping-1","ping-2"]]
  ```

  `waitForFollowupQueueDrain` console output — three live cases against the real `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` map, including the timeout path that returns `drained:false` with the right `remaining` count:

  ```
  [empty]         {"drained":true,"remaining":0} elapsed=0ms
  [busy->drained] {"drained":true,"remaining":0} elapsed=151ms
  [timeout]       {"drained":false,"remaining":2} elapsed=200ms
  ```

  Real test runner output for the changed files (vitest reports the actual function counts; pnpm `test-projects.mjs` shards by project):

  ```
  [test] starting test/vitest/vitest.cli.config.ts
   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  24 passed (24)
     Duration  2.21s
  [test] starting test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply.config.ts
   Test Files  2 passed (2)
        Tests  71 passed (71)
     Duration  4.05s
  [test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 11.96s
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: `flushAllInboundDebouncers` deterministically drains buffered Signal-style messages in 1 ms while the natural debounce window is 2500 ms, and reports the right buffer count (`flushedCount=3` for two keys in one debouncer plus one in another). `waitForFollowupQueueDrain` returns immediately when the registry is empty, observes the queue clearing in real time during the busy case (151 ms — one poll interval past the 120 ms cleanup), and correctly returns `drained:false` with `remaining=2` when the timeout fires before drain. The CLI banner reports `(5d1e659)` confirming the built binary contains the patched commit. With the SIGUSR1 hook in `src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts` calling these helpers in the right order before `markGatewayDraining()`, late inbound messages are pushed into followup queues and given up to 5 s to drain instead of being silently dropped.
- **What was not tested**: An end-to-end real-Signal SIGUSR1 reproduction was not executed on this machine (no signal-cli account paired here); the production restart path is exercised through `src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts`, but a live Signal → `config.patch` → restart roundtrip on real `signal-cli` was not run. Behavior under simultaneous restart-while-flushing on a hung `onFlush` (the registered handle is intentionally retained on flush failure for a future sweep) was not exercised live; that path is covered only by the unit tests in `src/auto-reply/inbound.test.ts`.

target.title: [Bug]: opencode-go provider streaming — API calls complete on provider side but gateway never receives stream termination signal
target.body:
## Summary

opencode-go routes through the shared OpenAI-compatible completions provider. When the provider emits tokens but the upstream SSE socket then stalls (never sends `[DONE]`, never closes the stream), the gateway sits on `model_call:stream_progress` until `stuckSessionAbortMs` (~622s) aborts it, surfacing as `LLM request failed.` / `Request was aborted`. Issue #93610 reports ~90% of opencode-go cron jobs failing this way intermittently.

This PR adds a **provider-owned, abortable** stream wrapper at the raw opencode-go SSE boundary — the direction requested in the linked PR threads (#93640 / #93655 review comments). It does not touch the shared `openai-completions.ts` provider or any shared stream consumer.

### Behavior

- **Provider-scoped.** Only activates when `model.provider === "opencode-go"`. Other providers pass through unchanged.
- **Abortable at the raw SSE boundary.** The wrapper injects a fresh `AbortController` into the underlying OpenAI SDK request via `options.signal` (which `openai-completions.ts` already forwards as `requestOptions.signal`). When the wrapper detects a stall, it calls `controller.abort()`, which genuinely interrupts the underlying fetch/stream — not just `iterator.return()`.
- **Idle-based termination.** A configurable idle window (default 120s — matches the runtime's shared `DEFAULT_LLM_IDLE_TIMEOUT_MS`, far below the 622s stuck-session recovery, far above normal delayed usage-only chunk gaps) arms only after the first forwarded upstream event, then refreshes on every subsequent forward-progress event (text/tool/thinking delta, usage-only chunk). A natural `done`/`error` event cancels it. The pre-progress window (request → first event) is left to the runtime's own first-event watchdog, which cron runs deliberately leave uncapped (`resolveLlmIdleTimeoutMs` returns 0 when `trigger === "cron"`). So:
  - **Genuinely stalled stream** → aborted at ~120s with a downstream `{ type: "error", reason: "aborted" }` event, well before the 622s recovery would fire.
  - **Normal delayed / usage-only completion** → preserved unchanged. As long as chunks keep arriving (even slowly) the timer keeps refreshing; the final `done` event cancels it.

### Why provider-scoped, not in the shared runtime

A stall at the OpenAI SSE layer can have provider-specific causes (opencode-go's DeepSeek/Kimi/GLM routing through Zen vs Go catalogs has its own SSE quirks). Fixing this in the shared runtime would impose a new idle budget on every OpenAI-compatible provider, including ones with legitimately long thinking windows. Keeping the wrapper inside `extensions/opencode-go/` lets opencode-go tune its own threshold without coupling to the shared runtime contract. The default 120s is chosen to exactly match the runtime's shared `DEFAULT_LLM_IDLE_TIMEOUT_MS`, so non-cron interactive opencode-go runs see no behavior change versus the existing watchdog; cron runs — for which the runtime disables its idle watchdog entirely (`resolveLlmIdleTimeoutMs` returns 0 when `trigger === "cron"` and no explicit timeout is set) — finally get provider-owned termination well before the ~622s stuck-session recovery kicks in.

### Root cause chain

1. opencode-go plugin's `wrapStreamFn` (in `extensions/opencode-go/stream.ts`) previously only wrapped payload/reasoning mutation helpers. It had no raw SSE consumer.
2. The actual stream came from the shared `src/llm/providers/openai-completions.ts:337`, where `for await (const chunk of openaiStream)` blocks indefinitely when the provider's SSE socket emits tokens and then never closes.
3. `AssistantMessageEventStream` (`packages/llm-core/src/utils/event-stream.ts`) only exposes `push`/`end`/`result`. It has no `abort`; it relies on the underlying iterator finishing naturally.
4. The only abortable surface in this chain is `options.signal` (an `AbortSignal`), which `openai-completions.ts:168` forwards to the OpenAI SDK `requestOptions.signal`. Today only the outer stuck-session recovery (622s) triggers it.
5. Therefore the provider-owned fix has to live in `extensions/opencode-go/stream.ts`'s `wrapStreamFn`, where it can both inject an `AbortController` into `options.signal` before calling the underlying stream and monitor the wrapped stream's event cadence to abort when progress stalls.

## Linked context

- Issue: #93610 (`[Bug]: opencode-go provider streaming — API calls complete on provider side but gateway never receives stream termination signal`).
- Related PRs closed in favor of this direction: #93640, #93655, #93708 (the shared-runtime/compat-shim approach was rejected; maintainer asked for a provider-scoped and abortable fix at the raw opencode-go SSE boundary).
- Provider-owned hook used: `wrapStreamFn` (`extensions/opencode-go/index.ts`).
- Shared files intentionally untouched: `src/llm/providers/openai-completions.ts`, `packages/llm-core/src/utils/event-stream.ts`, `src/plugin-sdk/provider-stream-shared.ts`.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- behavior or issue addressed: opencode-go cron jobs hang for ~622s on `model_call:stream_progress` after the provider has emitted tokens but never closes the SSE stream. The gateway then aborts via `stuckSessionAbortMs` and surfaces `LLM request failed.` / `Request was aborted`. Issue #93610 reports ~90% of opencode-go cron jobs failing intermittently this way.
- real environment tested: in-repo vitest harness for the `extension-misc` shard (the same shard CI runs). A live opencode-go/DeepSeek run would require an `OPENCODE_API_KEY` and would not be deterministic for a stalled SSE (issue #93610 reports it as intermittent, ~90% on the reporter's cron jobs but not 100%). The unit suite at the `wrapStreamFn` boundary is the deterministic way to prove both the stall-then-abort path and the normal delayed completion path; production opencode-go streams go through this same wrapper.
- exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-misc.config.ts extensions/opencode-go/` (Exit code: 0)
- evidence after fix: terminal capture below — 19 tests in 5 files pass, including the three boundary tests in `stream-termination.test.ts`.

```text
RUN  v4.1.8 [redacted-local-path]

 Test Files  5 passed (5)
      Tests  19 passed (19)
   Start at  16:07:27
   Duration  2.71s
```

Boundary tests (deterministic, fake-timer driven):

1. Stalled stream is aborted — a fake base stream emits `start` + one `text_delta` (proving the provider produced tokens) then nothing; with `idleTimeoutMs=5000`, advancing fake timers past 5s triggers `controller.abort()` on the injected `AbortSignal` (verified via `signal.addEventListener("abort", ...)`) and the downstream consumer receives a terminal `{ type: "error", reason: "aborted" }` event.
2. Slow time-to-first-byte is not mis-aborted — a fake base stream emits nothing; advancing fake timers past 60s (well beyond the 5s idle timeout) shows `abort()` was NOT called and the downstream consumer received zero events. The idle timer is armed only after the first forwarded upstream event, so the pre-progress window stays governed by the runtime's own first-event watchdog (which cron runs deliberately leave uncapped via `resolveLlmIdleTimeoutMs`).
3. Normal delayed completion is not truncated — a fake base stream emits `start` + `text_delta` + (2s gap, below the 5s idle timeout) + `done`; advancing fake timers past 10s after `done` shows `abort()` was NOT called and the `done` event is forwarded to downstream unchanged.

- Observed result after fix: stalled path — AbortSignal fired, downstream received `{ type: "error", reason: "aborted" }` instead of hanging until 622s stuck-session recovery; normal delayed path — no abort, full `done` event forwarded.
- what was not tested: a real opencode-go/DeepSeek request requires an `OPENCODE_API_KEY` and the intermittent stall itself is non-deterministic; the live provider path is not exercised here. Local `tsgo` reports one pre-existing error in `extensions/opencode-go/provider-catalog.ts` (`Cannot find module 'openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-catalog-live-runtime'`) that is present on `main` as well — local-dist-missing artifact, resolved by CI's build step.
- Fix classification: root cause fix — provider-owned abortable SSE boundary, not a compat shim.
- Maintainer-ready confidence: high.
- Root cause: opencode-go's `wrapStreamFn` never owned the raw SSE boundary, so the shared `openai-completions.ts` `for await (chunk of openaiStream)` loop blocked indefinitely whenever the upstream provider emitted tokens and then never closed the stream; the only abortable surface is `options.signal` flowing into the OpenAI SDK `requestOptions.signal`, and only the 622s stuck-session recovery could trigger it. The upstream invariant ("provider streams terminate") is violated by opencode-go's DeepSeek/Kimi/GLM SSE quirks, so the fix has to live at the opencode-go provider boundary, where it can both inject an AbortController and observe event cadence to abort when forward progress stalls.
- Why this is root-cause fix: because the wrapper is the only layer that can both (a) inject an AbortController into `options.signal` before calling the underlying stream (interrupting the real OpenAI SDK fetch, not just `iterator.return()`) and (b) monitor the wrapped stream's event cadence to detect the actual stall condition (no forward progress between events). Putting the abort anywhere upstream (shared runtime) would impose the idle budget on every OpenAI-compatible provider; putting it downstream (event-stream consumer) cannot interrupt the OpenAI SDK fetch at all. The provider boundary is the only correct owner of this contract.
- What did NOT change: shared runtime files unchanged (no unrelated changes to `src/llm/providers/openai-completions.ts`, `packages/llm-core/src/utils/event-stream.ts`, `src/plugin-sdk/provider-stream-shared.ts`); other providers' stream behavior unchanged (early return when `model.provider !== "opencode-go"`); no new dependency on the shared runtime; no schema/config/migration/API changes.

## Tests and validation

- Extension-specific test: new file `extensions/opencode-go/stream-termination.test.ts` (vitest, runs in the `extension-misc` shard alongside the existing opencode-go suite).
- The wrapper is also wired through `createOpencodeGoWrapper` in `extensions/opencode-go/stream.ts`, and the full 19-test opencode-go extension suite passes with the new layer in place — no regressions in the existing Kimi reasoning sanitizer, DeepSeek V4 thinking, onboarding, or media-understanding tests.
- The wrapper does not change `openai-completions.ts` or any other shared runtime file.

## Risk checklist

- [x] **Provider-scoped only.** Non-opencode-go providers are not affected (early return at the top of the wrapped streamFn).
- [x] **No truncation of normal completion.** Idle timer refreshes on every event (including delayed usage-only chunks) and is canceled on natural `done`/`error`.
- [x] **No new coupling to the shared runtime.** Wrapper lives entirely in `extensions/opencode-go/`.
- [x] **Threshold tuning.** Default 120s — matches the runtime's shared `DEFAULT_LLM_IDLE_TIMEOUT_MS`, so non-cron interactive opencode-go runs see no behavior change versus the existing watchdog, while cron runs (for which the runtime disables its idle watchdog entirely) finally get provider-owned termination well before the 622s stuck-session recovery. Can be tuned later via the `idleTimeoutMs` parameter without touching the shared runtime.
- [x] **No shared file changes.** `openai-completions.ts`, `event-stream.ts`, and `provider-stream-shared.ts` are intentionally untouched.
- [ ] **Live provider path not exercised.** See "What was not tested" above.
- [x] **Default 120s matches runtime `DEFAULT_LLM_IDLE_TIMEOUT_MS`.** Pre-submit Codex review on the prior 30s default flagged a regression risk for slow time-to-first-byte reasoning completions; the default now matches the runtime's shared `DEFAULT_LLM_IDLE_TIMEOUT_MS` exactly so non-cron interactive runs see no behavior change. The default is still far below the ~622s stuck-session recovery that cron runs hit today.

## Current review state

- Local: 19/19 vitest tests pass; tsgo clean for the new file (one pre-existing error in an unrelated module on `main`).
- Idle-timer lifecycle fix: the timer is armed only after the first forwarded upstream event (regression test added), addressing the ClawSweeper P1 finding that arming earlier would mis-abort slow time-to-first-byte requests that cron runs deliberately leave uncapped.
- Maintainer-directed direction: provider-scoped and abortable at the raw opencode-go SSE boundary.

Refs #93610

target.title: feat(feishu): comprehensive plugin enhancements — streaming, dedup, skills, calendar, and stability
target.body:
## Summary

This PR consolidates all Feishu plugin enhancements developed across multiple iterations (supersedes #38770, #38772, #39409, #39440). All previous PRs were closed without merging; this PR contains the complete changeset.

---

## Issues Fixed

### Streaming Card Display

1. **Text Duplication in Streaming Cards**: `mergeStreamingText()` fallback path duplicated cumulative text → bypass it, treat `onPartialReply` text as complete state
2. **Content Duplication Across Cards**: each card showed cumulative text instead of its own block → track `blockOffset`, slice cumulative text per card
3. **Content Disappearing on Card Close**: `closeStreaming()` used per-block text while card displayed cumulative text → `streamText` consistently uses sliced card text
4. **Missing Tail Text After Last Block**: `deliver(kind=final)` sent full duplicate or was skipped → extract only undelivered tail `text.slice(blockOffset)`, send if non-empty
5. **Extra Streaming Card After Last Block**: `onPartialReply` kept creating new cards → only start new card if substantial new content beyond `blockOffset`
6. **Slow Streaming Updates**: `await this.queue` blocked `onPartialReply` on API round-trips; no flush timer → remove `await` (fire-and-forget), add `pendingFlushTimer` (300ms auto-flush)
7. **Markdown Crash Guard**: `isSafeToRender()` blocks updates during unclosed code fences
8. **Typing Indicator Emoji**: Changed from `Typing` to `Get` (fix reaction 231001 error)
9. **Broken CardKit Streaming**: Replaced broken CardKit chunked patching with Semantic Block Delivery using `block` events from `reply-dispatcher.ts`

### Message Deduplication (3-Layer)

10. **event_id Dedup**: Lark SDK `EventDispatcher.parse()` flattens header into callback data — `event_id` IS available. Replaced content-hash workaround with proper `event_id` dedup as recommended by Feishu docs
11. **Stale Message Filtering in Debounce**: When debounce batch has messages with >2min time gap, drop older messages and record their IDs in persistent dedup
12. **create_time-based Stale Rejection**: Feishu retries re-deliver messages with new `message_id`/`event_id` but same `create_time`. Track last processed `create_time` per `chat+sender` and reject stale duplicates

### Message Control & Abort

13. **Fast Abort Bypass**: Trigger words (`stop`, `停止`, `取消`, `esc`, etc.) skip 2s debounce + bypass `chatQueue` → direct dispatch to `tryFastAbortFromMessage`
14. **Message Recall Abort**: Register `im.message.recalled_v1` → clear debounce buffer, record in dedup, synthesize `/stop` command via `dispatchFeishuMessageDirect`

### Feishu Ops Tools (Skills)

15. **feishu_contacts**: Search contacts with auto-sync from API
16. **feishu_groups**: List/search bot groups with auto-sync
17. **feishu_send**: Send text/file/@all to users or groups
18. **feishu_calendar**: Create, **update**, and **delete** calendar events by `event_id`
19. **2-Tier Contact Sync**: When list API returns 0 results, fallback to BFS department traversal + per-department user fetch (ported from `feishu-ops/scripts/update-contacts.py`)
20. **SKILL.md Rewrite**: All skill docs converted to English with JSON tool-call examples

### Code Quality

21. **Error Messages Standardized to English** across all new modules
22. **Group Search Hint**: `searchGroupsOrSync()` lists all bot groups and prompts user to add bot to target group if not found
23. **Mention Logic**: More resilient verify mention logic and fix log propagation
24. **SDK v1.30+ Response Parsing**: Fix `proactive-send.ts` to handle Lark SDK top-level response format (`{ file_key }` instead of `{ code: 0, data: { file_key } }`)

### Other

25. **Daemon Working Directory**: Resolve gateway working directory to installation root

---

## Files Changed

| File | Changes |
|---|---|
| `extensions/feishu/src/bot.ts` | event_id dedup, create_time dedup, fast abort bypass, recall handler, mention logic |
| `extensions/feishu/src/dedup.ts` | `checkAndRecordCreateTime()`, create_time tracking per chat+sender |
| `extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.ts` | Block-boundary card splitting, blockOffset tracking, tail text, semantic block delivery |
| `extensions/feishu/src/streaming-card.ts` | Remove mergeStreamingText, async queue, pendingFlushTimer, isSafeToRender |
| `extensions/feishu/src/typing.ts` | Change typing emoji to Get |
| `extensions/feishu/src/inbound-debounce.ts` | Stale message filtering, removeFromBuffer() |
| `extensions/feishu/src/feishu-ops-tools.ts` | Register contacts, groups, send, calendar tools |
| `extensions/feishu/src/contacts.ts` | 2-tier sync with department fallback |
| `extensions/feishu/src/calendar.ts` | Update and delete calendar events |
| `extensions/feishu/src/proactive-send.ts` | SDK v1.30+ response parsing fix |
| `extensions/feishu/skills/*/SKILL.md` | English rewrite with JSON examples |
| `src/daemon/program-args.ts` | Working directory resolution fix |

## Supersedes

- #38770 (CLOSED — streaming card fix)
- #38772 (CLOSED — streaming + event_id dedup)
- #39409 (CLOSED — message control, skills, stability)
- #39440 (CLOSED — calendar update/delete)

target.title: Gateway: allow extension origins in browser allowlist
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` could not explicitly allowlist `chrome-extension://` because the origin checker normalized extension origins through `URL.origin`, which becomes `"null"` for that scheme. - Why it matters: extension sidepanel clients had to rely on `["*"]` to get through browser-origin WS checks, which weakened the intended hardening. - What changed: the origin matcher now keeps exact URL parsing/validation but falls back to exact `scheme://host` matching for non-standard origins, so `chrome-extension://` can be allowlisted exactly; focused unit and handshake tests were added. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): no wildcard/prefix expansion, no acceptance of literal `Origin: null`, no changes to token/device auth, host-header fallback, or non-browser client auth behavior. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor - [ ] Docs - [x] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #35035 (issue mentions app://* origin, but it's the same underlying problem) - Related #35396, #35057 (both PRS were fixes for this problem but were closed because the authors were banned AFAIK) ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` can now contain exact extension origins such as `chrome-extension://abcdefghijklmnop`, and those browser-origin Gateway WS connects will succeed when otherwise authenticated. - Operators no longer need `["*"]` solely to let a Chrome extension sidepanel client connect. - Existing exact matches for normal `http(s)` origins, wildcard behavior, host-header fallback, and literal `Origin: null` rejection remain unchanged. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` (strictly speaking this allows chrome extensions/electron+tauri apps to connect but I think calling it a new capability is a stretch) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: `N/A` ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: local Bun + Vitest - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): Gateway WS browser-origin client; Chrome extension origin emulated in handshake tests via `ws` - Relevant config (redacted): `gateway.auth.mode="token"`, `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins=["chrome-extension://abcdefghijklmnop"]` ### Steps 1. Configure the Gateway with token auth and `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins=["chrome-extension://abcdefghijklmnop"]`. 2. Open a WS connection that sends `Origin: chrome-extension://abcdefghijklmnop` and a valid `connect` request. 3. Observe the handshake result. ### Expected - An exactly allowlisted extension origin should be accepted, just like an exactly allowlisted `https://...` origin. ### Actual - Before this fix, the request was rejected with `origin not allowed` because the checker collapsed the extension origin to `"null"` internally. - After this fix, the handshake succeeds and returns `hello-ok`. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: whitelisted the chrome extension I'm developing and successfully connected to the Gateway via WS - Edge cases checked: N/A - What you did **not** verify: did not try with other protocols (`app://`, `tauri://`) ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` - Config/env changes? `No` - Migration needed? `No` - If yes, exact upgrade steps: `N/A` ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this commit; if extension access is urgently blocked, temporarily fall back to `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins=["*"]` until the regression is understood. - Files/config to restore: `src/gateway/origin-check.ts`; optionally restore prior `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` values in config. - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: an exactly allowlisted `chrome-extension://` still being rejected; malformed/non-matching non-standard origins unexpectedly being accepted; regressions in existing `http(s)` origin allowlisting.

target.title: fix: CLI auth status/probe path regression
target.body:
## Summary

Found one regression in the CLI auth status/probe path. The commit intentionally makes unscoped external CLI discovery skip missing runtime-only profiles, but `models status` and `models status --probe` still load the auth store without passing the providers they are about to report or probe.

## What ClawSweeper Is Fixing

- **Medium: `models status --probe` misses CLI-only credentials after scoped discovery** (regression)
  - File: `src/commands/models/list.probe.ts:259`
  - Evidence: `src/agents/auth-profiles/external-cli-sync.ts:210` now returns false for unscoped missing external CLI profiles unless a matching stored OAuth profile already exists. `buildProbeTargets` still calls `ensureAuthProfileStore(agentDir)` without `allowKeychainPrompt: false`, `externalCliProviderIds`, or `externalCliProfileIds`, even though the function receives the exact `providers` and probe profile filters at `src/commands/models/list.probe.ts:254-256`. A focused smoke with a temporary Codex CLI auth file confirmed `resolveExternalCliAuthProfiles(emptyStore)` returns `[]`, while `resolveExternalCliAuthProfiles(emptyStore, { providerIds: ["openai-codex"] })` returns `["openai-codex:default"]`.
  - Impact: A user who only has file-backed Codex/Claude/Minimax CLI credentials and no persisted OpenClaw auth profile can resolve runtime auth through the newly scoped model-auth path, but `openclaw models status --probe` will build no profile target for that same credential. This produces false “nothing to probe” or missing-auth diagnostics for the provider most likely being checked.
  - Suggested fix: In `buildProbeTargets`, load the store with a scope derived from the requested probe surface, for example `ensureAuthProfileStore(agentDir, { allowKeychainPrompt: false, config: cfg, externalCliProviderIds: providers, externalCliProfileIds: options.profileIds })`. The plain status path at `src/commands/models/list.status-command.ts:223` should likewise pass the config-derived external CLI scope, matching `src/gateway/server-methods/models-auth-status.ts:296-301`.
  - Confidence: high

## Expected Repair Surface

- `src/commands/models/list.probe.ts`
- `src/agents/auth-profiles.external-cli-scope.test.ts`
- `src/agents/auth-profiles.external-cli-sync.test.ts`
- `src/agents/auth-profiles/external-cli-scope.ts`
- `src/agents/auth-profiles/external-cli-sync.ts`
- `src/agents/model-auth.profiles.test.ts`
- `src/agents/model-auth.ts`

## Source And Review Context

- ClawSweeper report: https://github.com/openclaw/clawsweeper/blob/main/records/openclaw-openclaw/commits/581fbea1d65355917886babffe4161f8b3178a12.md
- Commit under review: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/581fbea1d65355917886babffe4161f8b3178a12
- Latest main at intake: 581fbea1d65355917886babffe4161f8b3178a12
- Original commit author: Peter Steinberger
- GitHub author: @steipete
- Highest severity: medium

## Summary

Closes #72572.

This restores support for dotfile-managed `~/.openclaw` directories while keeping exec approval paths hardened on both the Node and macOS node-host paths.

- allow trusted first-level `~/.openclaw` symlinks on POSIX after validating link ownership, link location, target ownership, target permissions, and stable resolved ancestors

- apply equivalent state-dir, JSON store, and socket-parent hardening on macOS, including symlinked `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` overrides and system `/tmp`/`/var` symlink roots
- document the supported symlink shape in approvals CLI docs

target.title: fix(cron): warn on main heartbeat handoff ghost runs
target.body:
Fixes #63106.

## Summary

- Add a scoped warning for fast successful main-session `systemEvent` cron jobs using `wakeMode="next-heartbeat"`.
- Persist the `possible-main-next-heartbeat-ghost-run` annotation in cron run logs.
- Carry and render cron run-log warnings in the Control UI run history.
- Add `cron.ghostRunWarningThresholdMs` config with schema/help metadata and public docs.
- Keep `CronRunLogEntry.warnings` source-compatible in generated Swift by defaulting the optional initializer parameter to `nil`.
- Keep release-note context in the PR body only; this branch does not edit `CHANGELOG.md`.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Verified that a fast successful main-session `systemEvent` cron handoff using `wakeMode: "next-heartbeat"` writes the `possible-main-next-heartbeat-ghost-run` warning into the cron run log, that Control UI run history can render that warning from `CronRunLogEntry.warnings`, and that existing Swift call sites can construct `CronRunLogEntry` without passing the new optional `warnings` label.

The cron proof used an isolated temporary `OPENCLAW_HOME` and did not touch the real user cron store.

target.title: fix(config): add actionable hints to version-mismatch warning on downgrade
target.body:
## Problem

When a user downgrades from a newer OpenClaw version (e.g. 2026.4.24 → 2026.3.2), `openclaw.json` may contain keys and value formats written by the newer binary that are invalid in the older schema. The gateway currently emits:

> Config was last written by a newer OpenClaw (2026.4.24); current version is 2026.3.2.

...but gives no guidance on what to do next. Users are left with cryptic `Invalid config` errors and a non-functional Discord channel (see #72527).

## Fix

Extend `warnIfConfigFromFuture` to include actionable follow-up hints:

1. **Run `openclaw doctor --fix`** — this already calls `stripUnknownConfigKeys` which removes keys the current Zod schema doesn't recognise (e.g. `agents.defaults.imageGenerationModel` added in 2026.4.x).
2. **Note about value-format changes** — some known keys had their value format changed between versions (e.g. `channels.discord.streaming` migrated from a plain string to an object in 2026.4.x). These cannot be auto-stripped and require manual correction; the warning now says so explicitly.

## Before / After

**Before:**
```
Config was last written by a newer OpenClaw (2026.4.24); current version is 2026.3.2.
```

**After:**
```
Config was last written by a newer OpenClaw (2026.4.24); current version is 2026.3.2. Some config keys or value formats may be incompatible with this version. Run "openclaw doctor --fix" to strip unrecognized keys automatically. Keys whose value format changed between versions (e.g. channels.*.streaming) may require manual correction after stripping.
```

## Related

- Closes #72527
- The Windows ESM Discord crash that triggered real-world downgrade traffic: #71749 (fixed in 2026.4.25-beta.4)

## Testing

Existing `warnIfConfigFromFuture` call sites are covered by integration tests in `io.ts`. No behaviour change for the happy path (same-version or forward configs). The warning is only emitted when `shouldWarnOnTouchedVersion` returns `true`.
